name: Apsaras (celestial dancers)
shortDescription: Apsaras are celestial nymphs or dancers in Buddhist (and Hindu) mythology, known for their beauty, grace, and ability to entertain gods and humans.
description: Apsaras are beautiful, supernatural female beings in Buddhist (and Hindu) mythology. They are youthful and elegant, often depicted as dancing and singing, entertaining the gods in the heavens, particularly Indra's court. Their origins can be traced back to the churning of the Ocean of Milk in Hindu mythology, where they emerged as divine beings. In Buddhist contexts, they are often associated with temples and palaces, serving as guardians or attendants to deities. Apsaras are known for their enchanting beauty, skilled dancing, and musical talents. They are believed to possess the power to change their form and are often associated with fertility and good fortune. In Southeast Asian art and architecture, particularly at Angkor Wat, apsaras are prominent figures, adorning the walls with their graceful poses and intricate costumes. They represent a connection between the earthly and divine realms, symbolizing beauty, prosperity, and the pleasures of the celestial world. While not inherently good or evil, their actions can influence events in the mortal realm, and they are generally considered benevolent figures who bring joy and inspiration.
appearance: Beautiful, youthful women, often depicted with elaborate headdresses, jewelry, and flowing garments, in graceful dancing poses.

sources.title: The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism
sources.author: Robert E. Buswell Jr. and Donald S. Lopez Jr.

sources.title: Angkor: Heart of an Asian Empire
sources.author: Bruno Dagens
